hero.subtitle: Free, open-source, and privacy-first. No account needed — your data stays in your browser.

steps.title: How it works
steps.step1Text: Upload a PDF to auto-fill fields, or build from scratch
steps.step2Text: See the formatted result instantly — tweak content, fonts, spacing
steps.step3Text: Export a polished PDF that passes ATS checks

features.freeText: No subscriptions, no locked features, no upsells. Every template, font, and export option is free.
features.usText: Single-column layout, standard section order, no photos or graphics — everything structured for Greenhouse, Lever, Workday, and other ATS.
features.privacyText: Your resume data never leaves your browser. Stored in localStorage, no server, no account, no tracking.
features.openSourceText: Built on the OpenResume project. Full source code on GitHub — audit it, fork it, contribute back.

qa.title: Common Questions
qa.q1: What is a resume builder and how is it different from a template?
qa.a1p1: A template (Word, Google Docs) gives you a blank structure — you manually format every section, adjust spacing, fix bullet alignment. A resume builder handles layout automatically: you enter content, it renders a consistent, print-ready PDF.
qa.a1p2: ResumeFlow goes further: it enforces ATS-friendly structure (single column, standard headings, no photos), lets you swap fonts and spacing in one click, and includes a built-in parser so you can verify how an ATS will read your file before you apply.
qa.q2: How is ResumeFlow different from other free builders like Reactive Resume or FlowCV?
qa.a2p1: Most builders optimize for visual flexibility — multiple columns, graphics, color themes. ResumeFlow optimizes for parseability:
qa.a2Feature1Text: Single column only. Standard section order. No photo field. No decorative elements that confuse parsers. Every default choice is validated against Greenhouse, Lever, and Workday parsing behavior.
qa.a2Feature2Text: No account creation, no cloud sync, no analytics on your resume content. Data lives in your browser's localStorage. You can even use it offline after first load.
qa.q3: How does ResumeFlow handle my resume data?
qa.a3p1: All data you enter into the builder is processed and stored locally in your browser — it is never sent to or saved on any server. This means no one, including us, has access to your resume content. You can close the tab and come back later — your data stays on your device until you delete it or clear your browser cache. The only time information leaves your browser is when you click "Download PDF".
qa.q4: How can the resume parser help if I already have a resume?
qa.a4p1: If you already have a resume in PDF or Word, you don't need to rewrite it from scratch — upload it to the parser, and the service will show you how an Applicant Tracking System (ATS) used by most large companies sees it. The parser flags formatting issues invisible to the human eye but that prevent the bot from correctly reading your experience and skills — such as tables, columns, or non-standard fonts that can cause your resume to be rejected before a recruiter even sees it.

parser.pageDescription: Upload a resume PDF to see exactly what an Applicant Tracking System extracts. The more fields parsed correctly, the better your chances of passing automated screening.
parser.example1Desc: Sample resume from University of La Verne Career Center —
parser.addResumeAfter: to check how well it parses. At minimum, your name and email should be recognized accurately.
parser.selectExample: Select an example or upload your resume to see parsing results.

algorithm.title: How the Parser Works
algorithm.intro: Technical overview of the ResumeFlow parsing pipeline — four stages from raw PDF to structured JSON. Designed for single-column English resumes.
algorithm.step1Title: Step 1. Extract text items from PDF
algorithm.step1p1: PDF is a binary format per the
algorithm.step1p1After: . Raw content is encoded; we use Mozilla's
algorithm.step1p2: to decode and pull every text item with its metadata (x/y position, font weight, line breaks).
algorithm.step1p3: The table below shows {n} extracted text items. Each item carries content plus positional metadata — coordinates relative to the page origin (bottom-left = 0,0), bold flag, line-break flag.
algorithm.step2Issue1Text: A single logical token — like a phone number "(123) 456-7890" — often splits across multiple items due to font changes or PDF encoding quirks.
algorithm.step2Solution1Text: Merge adjacent items when horizontal gap is smaller than average character width. Distance = RightItemX₁ − LeftItemX₂. Average width = total item widths / total characters (excluding bold markers and line breaks).
algorithm.step2Issue2Text: Humans read line by line, using boldness and proximity to group related text. Items lack this structure — they're a flat list.
algorithm.step2Solution2Text: Reconstruct lines by clustering items with similar Y-coordinates, then order left-to-right. This restores the visual reading order.
algorithm.step3p1: Identify section headers by heuristic: bold text + short length. Everything following a header belongs to that section until the next header appears.
algorithm.step3p2: Detected sections ({n} total): headers shown in
algorithm.step4Sub1Text: For each target field (name, email, phone), candidate text lines are scored against a set of feature functions. Highest score wins.
algorithm.step4DynamicText: In the uploaded resume, the name field is likely "{name}" with a feature score of {score} out of 5.
algorithm.step4Sub4Text: Some sections like Work Experience contain repeating sub-items (each job is a subsection). The parser detects these by finding bold lines that don't match section headers, then groups subsequent lines until the next bold line or section boundary. Each subsection is scored independently against the feature functions.
algorithm.step4CoreDegree: Contains degree keyword (Associate, Bachelor, Master, PhD)
algorithm.step4CoreDate: Date keywords: year, month, season, Present
algorithm.step4CoreJobTitle: Contains title keyword (Analyst, Engineer, Intern, Manager)
algorithm.step4CoreCompany: Bold or doesn't match title/date patterns
algorithm.step4CoreProject: Bold or doesn't match date pattern
algorithm.conclusion: That covers the ResumeFlow parsing pipeline.
algorithm.attribution: Based on the OpenResume parser by Xitang Zhao (June 2023)

dataWeCollect.title: Data We Collect
dataWeCollect.description: Your resume content — name, experience, education, skills, and all other fields — is stored exclusively in your browser's localStorage. It never touches our servers. No account creation, no email required, no tracking of resume content.

ym.title: Yandex Metrica / RSYA
ym.desc: Aggregate, anonymized traffic statistics. IP addresses are masked. You can opt out via browser Do Not Track or by disabling JavaScript.

cf.title: Cloudflare Web Analytics
cf.desc: Privacy-first, no cookies, no personal data collected.

fonts.title: Fonts
fonts.desc: Self-hosted on our domain, no requests to Google Fonts.

github.title: GitHub
github.desc: Only if you click "View on GitHub" links.

yourRights.title: Your Rights
yourRights.description: Since we don't store your data, there's nothing to delete, export, or rectify on our end. Clearing your browser storage (or using the "Clear data" option in the builder) removes all local data.

service.title: Service
service.description: ResumeFlow (resumeflow.fun) is a free, open-source tool for creating and parsing resumes. Provided "as is" without warranties of any kind — express or implied.

yourContent.title: Your Content
yourContent.description: You own your resume data. We claim no rights to it. Data stays in your browser — we never see it, store it, or process it on our servers.

acceptableUse.title: Acceptable Use
acceptableUse.1: Do not use the parser to extract personal information from resumes you don't own or have permission to process.
acceptableUse.2: Do not attempt to overload, reverse-engineer, or circumvent any part of the service.
acceptableUse.3: Do not use the service for any illegal or unauthorized purpose.

openSource.title: Open Source
openSource.description: The source code is licensed under MIT. You are free to fork, modify, self-host, and redistribute per the license terms.

noLiability.title: No Liability
noLiability.description: We are not responsible for any damages arising from use or inability to use the service, including formatting issues, parsing errors, missed job opportunities, or data loss in browser storage.

changes.title: Changes
changes.description: We may update these terms. Continued use after changes constitutes acceptance.
